prompt_text: Stitch vs alternatives: what should I switch to?
response:
You first need to clarify **which “Stitch” you’re using**, because there are two very different products:

- **Google Stitch (formerly Galileo)** – an AI **UI design** tool from Google Labs that turns text/sketches into app screens.[1][2]  
- **Stitch Data (Talend Stitch)** – an **ETL / data integration** service for syncing data into warehouses.[4][5][6]

The best alternative depends entirely on which one you mean and what you’re trying to improve (design workflow vs. data pipelines). I’ll cover both; you can skip to the relevant section.

---

## If you mean **Google Stitch** (AI UI design)

**When to switch:**  
Switch if you need any of the following that Stitch struggles with:

- Predictable, scalable usage (beyond its free but capped credits).[1]  
- Stronger **brand control**, **design systems**, or **production-ready output**.[1][2]  
- A direct path from UI → production code or live app/site.[1][2]  

Stitch is strong at **free, fast idea exploration**: text-to-UI, multi-screen flows, Figma & code export, and watching designs evolve transparently.[1][2]  
It’s weaker for **large teams, brand-heavy work, or deployment**.[1][2]

### Quick decision guide (Stitch → what to switch to)

According to a detailed comparison of Stitch alternatives, you should first ask:  
**Do you mainly want designs, code, or a live product?**[2]

| Your main goal | Recommended switch | Why |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Better text‑to‑UI screens & flows** (same idea as Stitch, but more control) | **Banani** or **UX Pilot** | Very close to Stitch for describe → generate screens → iterate, with clearer credit/pricing and some stronger workflow features.[1][2][3] |
| **Work inside Figma & real design systems** | **Figma Make / Figma AI** | Prompt-to-mockups directly in Figma’s ecosystem; best if your team already lives in Figma and needs refinement & collaboration.[1][2] |
| **Super simple ideation from sketches/text** | **Uizard** | Easier and more “non-designer-friendly”; fast for basic screens, though less faithful and refined than Stitch.[1][2] |
| **End‑to‑end design workflows (beyond UI)** | **Claude Design** | Generates broader artifacts (prototypes, decks, marketing assets) and taps into design systems; better for full workflows, though Stitch wins for pure rapid design iteration.[1] |
| **Interactive prototypes / working apps, not just mockups** | **Lovable** | Focuses on functional app prototypes and live-ish experiences, not just static designs.[1][2][3] |
| **Design → frontend code pipeline** | **Locofy** or **Anima** | Convert Figma designs to production-ready front-end code, for teams whose bottleneck is implementation.[2] |
| **Live site / app from AI designs** | **Framer**, **Lovable**, **Bolt**, **v0** | Oriented toward shipping a live product quickly rather than only designing.[2] |

### If you like Stitch but hit its limits

Given your current use of Stitch (assuming that’s your context), you probably fall in one of these:

1. **You mainly explore UI ideas, then refine in Figma.**  
   - You can **stay on Stitch** for ideation and **add Figma Make** for system-aware refinement and collaboration.[1][2]  

2. **You want a near-drop‑in replacement with better pricing and scale.**  
   - **Switch to Banani**: it’s very similar to Stitch (text-to-UI, multi designs, AI chat editing, Figma/code export), but has **transparent credits** (1 credit = 1 generation) and **paid tiers for predictable usage**.[1][2]  
   - This is the closest 1:1 replacement if you like Stitch’s workflow but dislike the credit cap and lack of a paid, scalable plan.[1]  

3. **You care about brand consistency / design systems and collaboration.**  
   - **Switch to Figma Make (Figma AI)**: you gain all of Figma’s ecosystem, plugin support, versioning, and precise refinement tools, while still getting AI generation.[1][2]  

If you tell me:
- whether you’re a **founder / PM / designer / engineer**, and  
- whether your priority is **speed**, **brand quality**, or **shipping live**,  

I can recommend a single “best next tool” and a concrete migration workflow (e.g., Stitch → Figma → Locofy).

---

## If you mean **Stitch Data
